# A00G Devices, Designs, & Diagrams (S26)

## ESE 5160: IoT Edge Computing Deadline: Jan 26, 202611:59 PM EST

A **group** submission to be submitted on GitHub Classroom & Gradescope.  
**GitHub Classroom assignment:** [https://classroom.github.com/a/cCe6o3Q6](https://classroom.github.com/a/cCe6o3Q6)   
**Late-day requests:** [https://forms.gle/7hZVrXLZ51XiCExm8](https://forms.gle/7hZVrXLZ51XiCExm8)   
Course Webpage: [https://ese5160.seas.upenn.edu/](https://ese5160.seas.upenn.edu/)	  
Remember to read the [ESE5160 S26 Assignment README](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1DQJxfhTMBN1aE2uasZiKpqR0JOT77pXvb6tUPF7iKF8/edit?tab=t.0#heading=h.c5nyj2pvdk6p) before starting\!

**All the required questions are highlighted in green.**  
**Important notes are highlighted in yellow.**

# Learning Outcomes

* You will have formed a project idea meaningfully integrating the Internet into an electronic device.  
* You will have documented hardware and software requirements for your system that define what success means for your project.  
* You will have created a simple block diagram for quickly communicating your idea with technical or non-technical team members.  
* You will have made a detailed block diagram to understand the complexities of interconnecting your microcontroller with power systems, sensors, and actuators.

# Errata

This section includes commonly asked questions about the assignment. It will be updated continuously throughout the assignment’s lifecycle, so please check it regularly.

(Currently nothing)

# 

# Required Equipment

* Hardware  
  * Your laptop or PC  
* Software  
  * [Visual Studio Code](https://code.visualstudio.com/) (Recommended)  
  * [Git](https://git-scm.com/)

# 1\. Team Contract

Your first task: **find your teammate**. Most assignments will be in the same **group of two** for the entire semester. You can use Ed Discussion to find your team member. Give your group a fun (and appropriate) name.

| Note: Your teammate must be enrolled in class. You may NOT pair up with someone on the waitlist. Doing so may result in a 0 on this assignment. |
| :---- |

Having a Team Contract in place is important for setting and maintaining expectations. Below are a few questions taken and modified from this website: [Team Contract](https://web.mit.edu/6.005/www/fa15/projects/abcplayer/team-contract/). Answer these questions and add anything else you’d like to the contact.

1. When will you meet as a team during the week? Write down at least one day and time.  
2. How much time per week do you anticipate it will take to make the project successful?  
3. Is it acceptable for one team member to do more work to get the team an A?  
4. What will happen if someone does not follow through on a commitment (e.g., missing a deadline, not showing up to meetings)?  
5. What will you do if a team member is not doing their share of the work?  
6. How will you deal with different work habits of individual team members (e.g., some people like to get assignments done as early as possible; others like to work under the pressure of a deadline)?

**Submission**

* Update the [ESE5160 S26 Project Teams](https://docs.google.com/spreadsheets/d/1syz5GUsa76g8SGjmpaPpTopSz9UGnjASNTJbAsona40/edit?usp=sharing) document (in the class’ Google Drive folder) with your team information.   
* Create a Team Contract that you will reference throughout the semester. Put this in your GitHub repository after you complete [2\. GitHub Primer](#2.-github-primer)

# 2\. GitHub Primer {#2.-github-primer}

This class will use GitHub to host global repositories, which serve as shared workspaces for team projects. Git repositories make collaborative development more efficient by storing and managing code.

A global repository contains the latest code version, accessible to everyone. Each developer works on a personal local repository, which is a copy of the global repository. Local code files are standard files for your project, while the local repository tracks your official changes (commits).

When you edit code, you update your local repository with your changes. These updates remain private until you explicitly push them to the global repository, making them accessible to others.

GitHub servers will host these global repositories for our class. To get started, **accept the GitHub Classroom assignment using the link at the top of this document**. If you haven’t already, download and install [VS Code](https://code.visualstudio.com/), [Git](https://git-scm.com/) ([git tutorial](#heading=h.lh22tpflrsg0)), and any necessary tools for your system. VS Code is user-friendly, supports many extensions, and helps you visualize README markdown files and track commits.

In this class, we recommend using the command line to push and pull commits; please read this [A00G Git Tutorial S26](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1VFjlKxSfttzBYsUB4C3GuCAH6tuVmC0BGOFG_5suxYA/edit?tab=t.0#heading=h.vt0kykdrjyyx) and complete the following submission.

**Submission:**  
In your README.md file, update your personal information under section: 2\. GitHub Primer:  
Team member 1:  
Name:   
PennID:  
Email:  
Team member 2:  
Name:   
PennID:  
Email:

Commit the following in order (both you and your teammate need to update):

1. Team member 1: Update the personal information required in the README.md file and commit it with the message “Are you still there, \[team member 2’s name\]?”  
2. Team member 2: Add your personal information in the README.md file, and commit it with the message “I’m here\!”

# 3\. Device Description

## 3.1 What makes a good IoT device?

You’ll develop an Internet-connected device with sensors and actuators for this class. Remember, a sensor is how the microcontroller receives information about the world around it (ex: temperature, acceleration, magnetic strength, microphones) and an actuator acts on the world around it (ex: lights, sound, heating, cooling).

Take time to ideate and refine your device description. **Ask your friends for honest feedback on your idea.** What would they use? What are their pain points with IoT devices? Many IoT devices are on the market \- but only a few great ones\!

An idea from robotics is the 3 Ds, which describes tasks ripe for automation \- **dull, dirty, and dangerous**. Many modern technological solutions address problems that fall into one or more of these categories. Examining what problems around you can be described as such may give you ideas for a useful product.

Remember \- this should be a project idea complex enough for a graduate-level engineering course. Choosing buttons as the sensors and LEDs as actuators doesn't cut it. **Ask yourself: how can Internet connectivity elevate the functionality of my device?**

Review what IoT devices are out there. Some suggested websites:

* [The Verge](https://www.theverge.com/) (Check out their [CES 2025 coverage](https://www.theverge.com/2025/1/4/24307731/ces-2025-tvs-gaming-smart-home-wearables-news)\!)  
* [Hackaday](https://hackaday.com/tag/iot/)  
* \[Your favorite tech website\]  
* [Adafruit](https://www.adafruit.com/)  
* [Sparkfun](https://www.sparkfun.com/)

Review the previous semesters for inspiration: [https://ese5160.seas.upenn.edu/\#finalproject](https://ese5160.seas.upenn.edu/#finalproject) 

## 3.2 What’s possible with our MCU?

Review our wireless module’s datasheet, the Silicon Labs [SIWG917Y](https://www.silabs.com/documents/public/data-sheets/siwg917y-datasheet.pdf) (specifically, the [SIWG917Y121MGABA](https://www.silabs.com/wireless/wi-fi/siwx917y-wireless-modules/device.siwg917y121mga?tab=specs) module \- we’ll call it the **917** for short\!). What peripherals are available? For example, how many I2C ports? UARTs? Does it have DACs or ADCs? What about I2S for digital audio?

If you’d like, you can start playing around with the software components and pin mapping tools in [Simplicity Studio 6](https://www.silabs.com/software-and-tools/simplicity-studio) to see what’s possible.  
![][image1]

The sensors and actuators you choose must have components **and** development boards in stock. The sensors and actuators you choose for this class must be available on [DigiKey](https://www.digikey.com/), [Mouser](https://www.mouser.com/), [Sparkfun](https://www.sparkfun.com/), or [Adafruit](https://www.adafruit.com/). DigiKey and Mouser are electronics distribution websites \- we’ll purchase the chip components for your printed circuit board assemblies here. [Sparkfun](https://www.sparkfun.com/) and [Adafruit](https://www.adafruit.com/) manufacture many development boards and do an excellent job of documenting the hardware & firmware.

* Make sure to review the [A00G Components to Avoid S26](https://docs.google.com/spreadsheets/d/11cTKaPSLGAW5uzuQNCQNRKEsdG2OuSSq2uVZ1oDM4Rs/edit?gid=42173259#gid=42173259) document in the Google Drive folder.  
* You may be interested in using parts that already have drivers written and tested by SiLabs \- those drivers are shown here: [https://github.com/SiliconLabsSoftware/third\_party\_hw\_drivers\_extension](https://github.com/SiliconLabsSoftware/third_party_hw_drivers_extension)   
* Your SMT parts (chips soldered to the PCB) will be limited to parts available in [JLCPCB’s stock](https://jlcpcb.com/parts/in-stock-parts).

Remember the difference between **components** and **development boards**. Generally, components are isolated from the PCB and can be hard to work with directly \- they’re meant to be a smaller size so they can be soldered onto PCBs. Development boards have the desired component, plus any supporting components to make it work correctly. Dev boards are larger and are easier to work with. The image below on the left is a component; on the right is a development board.

| Component/IC | Development/Evaluation Board |
| :---: | :---: |
| ![][image2] | ![][image3] |

## 3.3 Device Requirements

* Must use the **SIWG917Y121MGABA** as the microcontroller and wireless communication IC  
* At least one component must use I2C communication  
* At least one sensor in your system  
* At least one actuator in your system  
* Graduate-level system complexity  
* A reasonable budget for the sensors & actuators  
  * <!-- ~$30 max for the sensors & actuators per PCB assembly (assuming chip component cost) -->  
  * If you need more budget, email the professor to request special permission. We like to see you go above and beyond\!  
* Runs from a single-cell Li-Ion battery (3.7V nominal voltage)  
  * [Your Battery for ESE5160](https://www.adafruit.com/product/1781)

**Consult the Teaching Team if you’re considering any of the following:**

* Additional microcontrollers  
* Cameras  
  * MCUs are limited in memory, so handing images from cameras can quickly overload the system or cause a poor response time.  
* Wireless Radios (other than Wi-Fi & BLE)  
* An alternate power source to the single-cell Li-ion battery

**Submission:** Describe your IoT project, incorporating the device requirements above. Imagine you are explaining the project to a person who does not have the same technical knowledge as you.  The Device Description must be part of the README markdown file in your GitHub repository.

# 4\. User Persona

Who’s going to use your device? Your 88-year-old grandmother with arthritis? A 5-year-old with a disability? Products address a broad spectrum of end users \- we want you to start thinking about who you’re building your IoT device for.

Create a **fictional** person that would use your product. What are their demographics? Are they young, old, male, female? Do they have disabilities? Do they live alone, with roommates, their families? Developing a user persona may make you re-work your device concept; that’s a good thing\! Typically, you’ll have more than one user persona for your product.

You can do more reading in the resources below, but here’s an example \- assuming the **IoT device is emergency lighting**. You can have fun with it\!

| ![][image4] | Name: Anita Nulaptop Age: A youthful 80 Gender: Female Education: Doctorate in Electrical Engineering (Grace Hopper’s BFF) Location: Boston, MA (cold never bothered me anyway) Living Situation: Lives with son’s family in a single-family house Hobbies: Knitting, Pinching cheeks, and (oddly) Fortnite Favorite brands: Ensure, Nvidia Frustrations: It’s 2025, why can’t my house be smart yet\!? |
| :---- | :---- |

Resources:

* [How to Create Product Personas \+ Examples](https://productschool.com/blog/user-experience/product-persona)   
* [How to Create a Persona and an End User Profile for Your Startup](https://medium.com/disciplined-entrepreneurship/how-to-create-a-persona-and-an-end-user-profile-for-your-startup-67a5bdd05a8b)

**Submission:** Build a user profile for your product and submit it in your README.md.

# 5\. Hardware Requirements Specification (HRS)

Your device concept is taking shape\! Now, you will develop **hardware requirements specification (SRS)**. Review the [Appendix](#4-hardware-requirements-specification:) of this document to see what is necessary when developing requirements for a project.

| Note: You may need to update your Device Description as you refine your system requirements. We will continue to return to the requirements specifications throughout the semester. |
| :---- |

**Submission:** Generate an HRS that details the critical hardware features of your device. The HRS must be part of the README.md file in your GitHub repository.

# 6\. Software Requirements Specification (SRS)

Next, develop your **software requirements specification (SRS)**. Review the [Appendix](#heading=h.ja4l2ac3djm0) of this document to see what is necessary when developing requirements for a project.

| Note: You may need to update your Device Description as you refine your system requirements. We will continue to return to the requirements specifications throughout the semester. |
| :---- |

**Submission:** Generate an SRS that details the critical software features of your device. The SRS must be part of the README.md file in your GitHub repository.

# 7\. Simple System Block Diagram

Generate a simple system diagram explaining your system's most essential parts. Include the 917 wireless module, the most critical sensors and actuators, and the power architecture.

All devices need a power source, but adding power supply lines to each component would clutter up the diagram. Rather, state the power source on the top left side, as shown with the **Battery** shape below. Don’t forget to add any voltage regulators required.

You can use any diagramming software to do this \- PowerPoint or [yEd](https://www.yworks.com/products/yed) are good options. Feel free to use any software you like \- just don’t do this part on paper by hand\! [Diagrams.net](https://app.diagrams.net/) is especially nice because you can link your source directly to your GitHub repository. The following is a simple diagram of an **IoT Soil Quality Monitor.**

![][image5]  
**Submission:** A simple diagram of the system (must be computer-made – no hand-drawn diagram will be accepted). Readability will be graded – ensure the diagram is easy to follow and read\! Commit the source file to the GitHub repository and include an image in the README file in your GitHub repository.

# 8\. Detailed System Block Diagram

The Detailed System Diagram gets into the small but crucial elements of design. You’ll need to read the datasheets for your microcontroller and peripheral devices to determine these connections. When making the diagram, you might need to add some components or change some interactions to make the system work or “make sense”. That is ok\! It is part of the iterative process. If you find a sensor or actuator missing and need to add something, update the other questions in your submission.

Remember to use [JLCPCB](https://jlcpcb.com/parts), [DigiKey](https://www.digikey.com/), [Mouser](https://www.mouser.com/), [Adafruit](https://www.adafruit.com/), and [Sparkfun](https://www.sparkfun.com/) to research components. They will link to the component datasheets, which give you helpful information on the chips.

| Note: You don’t need to find specific power ICs for this assignment. Just figure out the general voltage regulators you’ll need, such as a 3.3V buck or a 5V boost, and add it to the diagram. In a later assignment, we’ll add specific power regulation ICs to the Bill of Materials (BOM). |
| :---- |

A detailed diagram will show the components and their interaction, especially with the MCU. For each component, specify the **communication protocols** and the power source. Here is an example of a detailed system diagram.  
![][image6]

**Submission:** Provide a detailed system diagram (must be computer-made – no hand-drawn diagram will be accepted). Make sure to indicate how each part interacts with one another, especially with the MCU. Commit the source file to the GitHub repository and include an image in the README file in your GitHub repository.

# 

# **Appendix**

## 1 Requirements Specifications

Software and hardware requirements specifications help to ensure that the product meets the needs of users and stakeholders. In regulated industries like medical devices and aviation, they are mandated by governing bodies like the FDA and FAA for systems comprising hardware and software. They are also used widely in non-regulated industries. 

Significant benefits of requirement specifications are:

* Clearly defines what the software or hardware is expected to do  
* Seeks to minimize or eliminate ambiguity between requirements and implementation  
* Forces designers to think through the requirements before any design is undertaken, thereby minimizing the need for redesign  
* Provides a realistic basis for estimating project timelines, risks, and costs  
* Makes it possible to verify the software or hardware against predefined requirements

Hardware and software specifications can run into hundreds or even thousands of pages.  We will not be going to that extent for our final project; instead, we will use this opportunity to learn how to formulate **key** hardware and software requirements.

[NASA has an excellent checklist for writing good requirements](https://www.nasa.gov/reference/appendix-c-how-to-write-a-good-requirement/) if you’d like more guidance.

In addition, the following ESE5160 projects show excellent requirements & validation testing:

* [https://github.com/ese5160/a14g-final-submission-t17-team-gotcha](https://github.com/ese5160/a14g-final-submission-t17-team-gotcha)   
* [https://github.com/ese5160/a14g-final-submission-t04-ad-astra](https://github.com/ese5160/a14g-final-submission-t04-ad-astra)   
* [https://github.com/ese5160/a14g-final-submission-t03-outdoor-innovators](https://github.com/ese5160/a14g-final-submission-t03-outdoor-innovators)  
* [https://github.com/ese5160/a14g-final-submission-t18-dataracers](https://github.com/ese5160/a14g-final-submission-t18-dataracers) 

## 2 Requirement Guidelines

### 2.1 Requirements shall be:

* **Explicit** – Shall be clear and easy to understand  
* **Complete** – Shall have sufficient details  
* **Feasible** – It shall be technically feasible to implement each requirement  
* **Consistent** – Shall not contradict other requirements  
* **Accurate** – Shall accurately define the functionality  
* **Testable** – it shall be possible to test each requirement

### 2.2 Keywords to use:

Shall: Use ‘shall’ for mandatory requirements. E.g., the musical instrument shall generate 5 basic tones: 220 HZ, 247 Hz, 262 HZ, & 294 HZ

Shall not: Use ‘shall not’ to indicate absolute restriction or prohibition.  E.g., the device shall not be powered by Li-ion batteries for safety reasons

Should: Use ‘should’ for recommended but not mandatory requirements. E.g., The completed project should be able to generate 10 musical tones in addition to the 5 basic tones 

## 3 Software Requirements Specification

### 3.1 Definitions, Abbreviations

Here, you will define any special terms, acronyms, or abbreviations you plan to use for hardware

### 3.2 Functionality

Examples are given below.

| ID | Description |
| :---- | :---- |
| SRS-01 | The IMU 3-axis acceleration will be measured with 16-bit depth every 100 milliseconds \+/-10 milliseconds. |
| SRS-02 | The distance sensor shall operate and report values at least every .5 seconds. |
| SRS-03 | Upon non-nominal distance detected (i.e., the trap mechanism has changed at least 10 cm from the nominal range), the system shall be able to detect the change and alert the user promptly (within 5 seconds). |
| SRS-04 | Upon a request from the user, the system shall get an image from the internal camera and upload the image to the user system within 10s. |

## 4 Hardware Requirements Specification: {#4-hardware-requirements-specification:}

### 4.1 Definitions, Abbreviations

Here, you will define any special terms, acronyms, or abbreviations you plan to use for hardware

### 4.2 Functionality

Examples are given below.

| ID | Description |
| :---- | :---- |
| HRS-01 | A distance sensor shall be used for obstacle detection. The sensor shall detect obstacles at a maximum distance of at least 10 cm. |
| HRS-02 | A noisemaker shall be inside the trap with a strength of at least 55 dB. |
| HRS-03 | An electronic motor shall be used to reset the trap remotely and have a torque of 40 Nm in order to reset the trap mechanism. |
| HRS-04 | A camera sensor shall be used to capture images of the trap interior. The resolution shall be at least 480p. |

# Rubric

See the Gradescope assignment for the rubric details. For all questions, 0 points may be awarded if the submission is non-existent, very poorly done, or doesn’t compile (for firmware assignments).
